finding: A desire for certainty drives group A to negatively evaluate category-mismatched individuals and distribute fewer resources to them, but this occurs only when the categorization is functionally relevant, and can be induced in group B by increasing their need for closure.

Perceivers hold stereotypes about behavior based on environmental harshness and resource-scarcity cues; when a secondary categorical cue (race) is confounded with these environmental properties, the environmental information outweighs the categorical signal in predicting stereotypes.

Memories of transgressive actions become progressively less clear and vivid over time, mediated by psychological discomfort; this unethical amnesia is specific to self-relevant unethical acts and correlates with increased dishonesty in future decisions.

A brief online intervention targeting teachers' mindsets about discipline, framed around understanding students rather than punishment, reduces year-long suspension rates in half and bolsters perception of respect among previously suspended students.

Actors engage in uncalculating cooperation more when their decision process is observable; observers trust uncalculating cooperators more and are correct to do so, indicating uncalculating cooperation signals genuine trustworthiness through a reputational channel.

Allocators chronically overestimate how much the fairness of an allocation procedure will matter to receivers because they occupy a pre-allocation perspective that highlights procedure differences, while receivers adopt a post-allocation perspective where outcome magnitude dominates.
